title: Economics of Small Decisions
description: Thirty questions on the economics hiding inside ordinary choices — not interest rates and GDP, but the trade-offs you make every day. Covers **opportunity cost**, the **sunk cost fallacy**, **marginal vs. total cost**, **fixed vs. variable costs**, **incentives and their unintended consequences**, **scarcity and trade-offs**, **diminishing returns**, and why something *free* so often still has a cost. It mixes concept-spotting (single-choice), find-every-cost scenarios (multiple-choice), and quick marginal-cost and percentage calculations (numeric input). Each question pairs a tempting wrong answer with an explanation that teaches the underlying idea, and every question carries Wikipedia references so you can verify it and read further.
author: Quizbun

questions.title: What does *opportunity cost* mean?

options.text: The value of the single next-best alternative you give up when you make a choice
The total value of every other option you didn't choose, added together
The amount of cash you actually hand over for something
A cost you already paid and cannot recover

questions.explanation: Opportunity cost is the value of the **best single alternative you forgo** — not the sum of all the roads not taken, and not just the cash you spend. If choosing to study means giving up either an $80 shift or a $30 evening out, the opportunity cost is the *better* of those (the $80), because you could only have done one of them anyway. It also includes *implicit* costs, like the rent a firm gives up by using a building it already owns. This is why economists say the real cost of anything is what you give up to get it.

questions.title: You have one free evening. Your options are an $80 extra shift, a $50 concert, or relaxing at home (worth about $30 to you). You pick the concert. What is the *opportunity cost* of that choice?

options.text: $80 — the value of the best alternative (the shift) you gave up
$110 — the shift plus the relaxing, added together
$50 — what you paid for the concert ticket
$0 — you chose what you wanted, so nothing was lost

questions.explanation: Opportunity cost counts only the **single most valuable thing you sacrificed**, which here is the $80 shift — not the $110 sum of everything you skipped, and not the $50 ticket (that's an *explicit* out-of-pocket cost, a different thing). And it is never $0: choosing one use of a scarce evening always means surrendering its best alternative use. Separating the price you pay from the alternative you lose is the core move of thinking like an economist.

questions.title: Which of these involve a real *opportunity cost*?

options.text: Using your only free Saturday to pick up an extra paid shift, giving up the leisure
Spending $2,000 of savings on a holiday instead of investing it
A bakery using a building it already owns for its shop instead of renting it out
The $400 you spent on last year's unused gym membership and can't get back
Taking the only option available, when you truly have no alternative

questions.explanation: An opportunity cost exists whenever a scarce resource — your time, your money, even a building you own — has a valuable *alternative* use you must give up. The forgone rent on an owned building is a classic **implicit** opportunity cost that accountants miss but economists count. The two wrong options fail for opposite reasons: last year's gym fee is a **sunk cost** (already gone, no longer an alternative you can choose), and an option with no alternative has *nothing* to forgo, so its opportunity cost is zero.

questions.title: You go to a *free* outdoor concert: $0 entry, but you skip a 4-hour shift paying $15/hour and spend $12 on gas. What is the total economic cost of going, in dollars? (Enter a number.)
questions.explanation: Economic cost = **explicit costs you pay** + **opportunity cost of what you give up**. That's $12 of gas plus the $60 you could have earned (4 hours × $15), for **$72** — even though the ticket was free. The zero-dollar entry fee is exactly the kind of headline 'free' that hides a real cost, here the wages you forgo by spending the evening elsewhere.

questions.title: You bought a $15 movie ticket in advance, but by showtime you feel sick and would honestly enjoy staying home more. Which cost is *sunk*?

options.text: The $15 you already paid for the ticket — it's gone whether or not you attend
The enjoyment you would get from watching the film
The cost of medicine you might buy if you stay home
The bus fare to the cinema that you haven't paid yet

questions.explanation: A **sunk cost** is money, time, or effort already spent that you cannot get back *no matter what you choose next*. The $15 ticket is gone either way, so it should drop out of the stay-or-go decision entirely — the only live questions are how you'll feel and what each option now costs and gives you going forward. The unpaid bus fare is *not* sunk, because you can still avoid it by staying home.

questions.title: What is the *sunk cost fallacy*?

options.text: Continuing something because of resources already spent, rather than its future costs and benefits
Refusing to ever spend money on big, long-term projects
Always choosing whichever option is cheapest right now
Ignoring the future entirely when you make a decision

questions.explanation: Rational choices look *only* at future costs and benefits, because the past can't be undone. The fallacy is letting unrecoverable spending trap you — 'I've already put so much in, I can't stop now' — which leads to throwing good money after bad. It's nicknamed the **Concorde fallacy**, after the supersonic airliner whose development was pushed on long past the point of economic sense precisely because so much had already been invested.

questions.title: Fill in the missing words: throwing good money after bad — pressing on with a failing plan only because of the time or money already poured in — is the '___ ___ fallacy.' (Give the two words before *fallacy*.)
questions.explanation: The answer is **sunk cost**. The trap is treating an unrecoverable past expense as a reason to keep going, when a clear-eyed decision weighs only what happens *from now on*. Spotting it in daily life — finishing a bad meal because you paid for it, staying in a course you hate because of the fees — is one of the most practical payoffs of economic thinking.

questions.title: What is *marginal cost*?

options.text: The additional cost of producing one more unit
The total cost of everything produced so far
The average cost per unit across the whole batch
The profit earned on the last unit sold

questions.explanation: Marginal cost is the *extra* cost of one more unit — the change in total cost when output rises by one. It is deliberately different from **total cost** (the running sum) and **average cost** (total ÷ quantity). Marginal cost is the number that should drive 'should I make/buy/do one *more*?' decisions, because only the next unit's cost is still in your control.

questions.title: A gym charges a flat $40/month for unlimited visits, with no per-visit fee. Ignoring travel, what is the marginal cost (in dollars) of going one extra time this month? (Enter a number.)
questions.explanation: The $40 is a **fixed** monthly cost: you owe it whether you visit zero times or thirty. So one *additional* visit adds no extra charge — its marginal cost is **$0**. This is the whole point of the example: people picture a per-visit cost by dividing $40 by the number of visits, but that's an *average*, not a *marginal*, cost. Because the marginal cost of going is essentially zero, once you've paid the membership you should go whenever the benefit is even slightly positive.

questions.title: A bakery's total cost to bake 50 loaves is $120. To bake 51 loaves it is $122. What is the marginal cost (in dollars) of that 51st loaf? (Enter a number.)
questions.explanation: Marginal cost is the change in total cost for one more unit: $122 − $120 = **$2**. Notice it differs from the *average* cost of a loaf so far ($120 ÷ 50 = $2.40). Because this 51st loaf costs less than the running average, baking it actually pulls the average cost down — a concrete example of why marginal and average cost are not the same number, and why decisions should ride on the margin.

questions.title: A workshop can make 100 chairs for a total cost of $4,000, or 120 chairs for a total cost of $4,500. Over that range, what is the marginal cost per additional chair (in dollars)? (Enter a number.)
questions.explanation: When output jumps by more than one unit, marginal cost is the change in total cost divided by the change in quantity: ($4,500 − $4,000) ÷ (120 − 100) = $500 ÷ 20 = **$25** per chair. This 'extra cost ÷ extra units' is exactly the comparison you want when deciding whether a bigger production run is worth it.

questions.title: Economists say good decisions are made 'at the margin.' What does that mean?

options.text: You compare the extra benefit of one more unit with its extra (marginal) cost, ignoring sunk totals
You always pick the option with the lowest total cost
You only act when something is completely free
You divide total cost by quantity and compare the averages

questions.explanation: Thinking at the margin means asking about the *next* unit: is the additional benefit of one more — one more hour of study, one more cup of coffee, one more item produced — worth its additional cost? You keep going while marginal benefit exceeds marginal cost and stop when they cross. This beats fixating on totals or averages, and it automatically ignores sunk costs, since those don't change with the next unit.

questions.title: Which is the best example of a *fixed cost* for a small café?

options.text: The monthly rent on the café space
The coffee beans used per cup
The milk poured into each latte
The hourly pay for extra weekend staff

questions.explanation: A **fixed cost** doesn't change with how much you produce in the short run: the café owes the same rent whether it sells 1 cup or 1,000. The beans, milk, and extra hourly staff all scale up the more you serve, so they're *variable* costs. Fixed costs are why a quiet day still 'costs' a lot — the meter is running regardless of sales.

questions.title: For that same café, which of these are *variable costs* that rise as it serves more cups?

options.text: Coffee beans
Milk
Disposable cups and lids
The fixed monthly rent
A flat annual insurance premium paid up front

questions.explanation: **Variable costs** rise and fall with output — every extra cup needs more beans, more milk, and another cup-and-lid. **Fixed costs** like rent and a flat annual insurance premium stay the same no matter how busy the café is. The two together make up total cost: **total cost = fixed costs + variable costs**, which is why understanding the split tells you how profit reacts as sales grow.

questions.title: Fill in the blank: in the short run, a firm's **total cost** equals its fixed costs plus its ___ costs.
questions.explanation: Total cost = fixed costs + **variable** costs. As output rises, the fixed part stays flat while the variable part grows, so the *average* cost per unit usually falls at first — you're spreading the fixed cost over more units — before rising again once variable costs climb steeply. Seeing total cost as these two pieces is what makes 'is one more unit worth it?' answerable.

questions.title: A government offers a cash bounty for every dead cobra, hoping to shrink the cobra population — but people start *breeding* cobras to cash in, and the population grows. An incentive that backfires like this is called a...?

options.text: Perverse incentive (the 'cobra effect')
Sunk cost
Fixed cost
Opportunity cost

questions.explanation: A **perverse incentive** produces a result opposite to what its designers intended, because people respond to the reward as written rather than its spirit. This one is nicknamed the *cobra effect* (a term coined by economist Horst Siebert). The cobra story's literal history is actually disputed — but a well-documented real case is the 1902 Hanoi rat-tail bounty under French colonial rule, where catchers bred rats and even released tail-less ones to keep the bounties coming. The lesson stands: design incentives carefully, because people optimize for exactly what you measure.

questions.title: In economics, what is an *incentive*?

options.text: Something that motivates a behavior by changing the costs or benefits people face
A tax the government collects each year
A cost that has already been spent
The total amount a firm produces

questions.explanation: An incentive is any reward or penalty that shifts the costs and benefits of an action and so nudges behavior — a discount, a fine, a bonus, a subsidy. A central idea of economics is that **people respond to incentives**, often in ways the designer didn't fully anticipate, which is the seed of unintended consequences.

questions.title: Which of these are examples of *incentives producing unintended consequences*?

options.text: Paying programmers per line of code, which encourages bloated, padded code
A rat-tail bounty that leads people to farm rats for their tails
Tying a teacher's bonus only to test scores, which encourages narrow teaching to the test
A per-unit sales tax that raises the price buyers pay
A discount that successfully increases sales of a product

questions.explanation: Unintended consequences appear when people chase the *letter* of an incentive in ways its designer didn't foresee: more code instead of better software, more rats instead of fewer, test prep instead of real learning. This is close to **Goodhart's law** — 'when a measure becomes a target, it stops being a good measure.' By contrast, a sales tax raising prices and a discount lifting sales are the *intended, predictable* effects of those incentives, not backfires.

questions.title: In economics, *scarcity* refers to...?

options.text: Limited resources relative to effectively unlimited wants, which forces us to choose
A temporary shortage caused by a delivery delay
Something being rare and therefore expensive, like a diamond
Running out of money at the end of the month

questions.explanation: Scarcity is the basic economic condition: our time, money, and resources are finite, but our wants are essentially limitless, so we *cannot have everything* and must choose. It's broader than a one-off shortage or a single rare item — it's the permanent backdrop that *gives rise to* trade-offs and opportunity costs in the first place. No scarcity, no economics.

questions.title: A country with a fixed budget can build more hospitals *or* more tanks, but not the maximum of both. This classic illustration of a *trade-off* is known as...?

options.text: The guns-versus-butter model
The sunk cost fallacy
The cobra effect
Diminishing marginal utility

questions.explanation: The **guns-versus-butter** model is the textbook picture of a trade-off under scarcity: with resources fully used, producing more 'guns' (military goods) means producing less 'butter' (civilian goods). Each extra tank carries an opportunity cost measured in the hospitals and schools you didn't build. It's the same logic as your personal budget — every dollar or hour committed here is unavailable there.

questions.title: What is the relationship between a *trade-off* and an *opportunity cost*?

options.text: A trade-off is giving up one thing for another; the opportunity cost is the value of the best thing given up
They are exactly the same thing under two names
A trade-off involves only money; opportunity cost involves only time
Opportunity cost applies to firms; trade-offs apply only to individuals

questions.explanation: They're tightly linked but distinct. A **trade-off** is the fact that, under scarcity, choosing more of one thing means accepting less of another — you can't have it all. The **opportunity cost** puts a *value* on that sacrifice: it's the worth of the single best alternative you gave up. So every choice is a trade-off, and its opportunity cost is how economists price the trade-off.

questions.title: The phrase 'there's no such thing as a free lunch' (TANSTAAFL) makes which economic point?

options.text: Even things that look free carry a cost — borne by someone, somewhere, or as forgone alternatives
Restaurants should never give food away
Lunch is the most important purchase of the day
Anything offered for free is always a scam

questions.explanation: The saying comes from 19th-century American saloons that gave a 'free' lunch to anyone buying a drink — the salty food simply sold more beer, so the lunch was paid for indirectly. Popularized by economist Milton Friedman, TANSTAAFL captures that because resources are scarce, a genuine benefit to one person is almost always paid for somewhere — in money, in someone else's cost, or in the opportunities you forgo to get it. It's not that free offers are scams; it's that the cost is just hidden or shifted.

questions.title: A phone app costs you no money to download or use. What is the most likely *hidden cost*?

options.text: Your time, attention, and personal data, which the app monetizes (often through ads)
There is genuinely no cost of any kind
Only the electricity used to charge your phone, and nothing more
A secret monthly fee the app charges you automatically

questions.explanation: When you don't pay with money, you usually pay with attention and data — hence the saying, 'if you're not paying for the product, you *are* the product.' Even ignoring data, the hours you spend have an opportunity cost: that time could go to something else you value. A free app isn't a secret subscription, but it isn't costless either — the price is just not denominated in dollars.

questions.title: A supermarket sells milk below its own cost to pull shoppers in, betting they'll fill a basket with profitable items too. This pricing tactic is called a...?

options.text: Loss leader
Sunk cost
Fixed cost
Perverse incentive

questions.explanation: A **loss leader** is an item priced below cost on purpose, to attract customers who then buy enough higher-margin goods to make the trip profitable for the store. Staples like milk or bananas are common loss leaders, often placed at the back so you walk past tempting products to reach them. It's a reminder that a striking 'bargain' is usually one move in the seller's larger cost-and-benefit calculation — the cheapness has a purpose.

questions.title: The *law of diminishing returns* says that...?

options.text: As you keep adding more of one input (holding others fixed), the extra output from each added unit eventually falls
Output always falls the moment you add any input
The more you produce, the cheaper each unit becomes forever
Consumers enjoy each extra unit they buy more than the last

questions.explanation: Diminishing returns is about *production*: when you add more of one input while other factors stay fixed — more cooks in one small kitchen — total output still rises, but each additional cook adds *less* than the one before, because they start getting in each other's way. The key word is **marginal**: it's the extra output per added unit that shrinks, not necessarily the total.

questions.title: On a fixed plot of land, 1 worker harvests 10 baskets, 2 workers harvest 18 total, and 3 workers harvest 24 total. What is the *marginal product* (extra baskets) of the 3rd worker? (Enter a number.)
questions.explanation: Marginal product is how much output the *next* worker adds: 24 − 18 = **6** baskets. Track the series: the 1st worker adds 10, the 2nd adds 8 (18 − 10), the 3rd adds 6. Output keeps rising, but by smaller and smaller steps on the same fixed plot — that shrinking sequence of marginal gains *is* diminishing returns in action.

questions.title: The first slice of pizza when you're hungry is delicious; the fourth is just okay; the sixth feels like a chore. This is best described as...?

options.text: Diminishing marginal utility
The sunk cost fallacy
A fixed cost
A perverse incentive

questions.explanation: **Diminishing marginal utility** is the consumption-side cousin of diminishing returns: each additional unit you consume adds *less* satisfaction than the one before. It's why you stop buying more once the value of the next slice drops below its price — a marginal calculation you make without thinking. Keep the distinction straight: diminishing *returns* is about adding inputs to *production*, while diminishing marginal *utility* is about *consuming* more units.

questions.title: Which of these are everyday examples of *diminishing returns or diminishing marginal utility*?

options.text: The 3rd coffee of the day gives you far less of a boost than the 1st
Cramming a 5th straight hour of study adds far fewer points than the 1st hour did
Adding a 6th cook to a tiny kitchen barely increases meals served
Doubling a recipe exactly doubles every ingredient you need
A monthly rent that stays the same no matter how much you produce

questions.explanation: Diminishing returns and diminishing marginal utility both mean each *additional* unit — of input or of consumption — yields a smaller *extra* gain: the 3rd coffee, the 5th study hour, the 6th cook in a cramped kitchen. The two wrong options are different ideas: doubling a recipe is simple **proportional** scaling (constant returns), and unchanging rent is a **fixed cost** — neither shows a shrinking marginal gain.

questions.title: A coffee shop offers unlimited coffee for $40/month. You'd otherwise buy your usual 25 coffees a month at $2 each ($50). What percentage do you save per month by subscribing? (Enter the number only, e.g. 20 for 20%.)
questions.explanation: Buying individually costs 25 × $2 = $50; the subscription costs $40, so you save $10, which is $10 ÷ $50 = **20%**. But there's a catch worth noticing: once you've paid the flat $40, the *marginal* cost of one more cup is $0, which can tempt you to drink past the point where the extra coffee is actually worth it (diminishing marginal utility). A genuine saving only materializes if your real consumption makes the flat fee cheaper than paying per cup.

questions.title: Which question best captures *thinking like an economist* about an everyday choice?

options.text: What do I give up by choosing this, and is the extra benefit worth the extra cost?
How much have I already spent on this so far?
What is the grand total cost of absolutely everything, added up?
What would other people think of my choice?

questions.explanation: Economic thinking is **forward-looking and marginal**. It asks two questions at once: *what's the opportunity cost* (the best alternative I'm giving up?) and *does the marginal benefit of this next step beat its marginal cost?* It deliberately ignores sunk costs ('how much I've already spent') and resists fixating on grand totals. Master those two habits and most of the concepts in this quiz — scarcity, trade-offs, marginal cost, sunk cost — become a single way of seeing choices.
